evidence: README.md delegates governance to multiple documents (`goseg/protocol/contracts/GOVERNANCE.md`, `goseg/uploadsvc/GOVERNANCE.md`, `goseg/system/wifi/README.md`, `goseg/startram/GOVERNANCE.md`), but assigned contract files expose only ID/name/description/message fields and local ID-prefix validation.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` defines protocol action literals and validates formula/prefix consistency internally, while runtime behavior in `goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` depends on `groundseg/protocol/actions` constants, creating cross-module synchronization by convention rather than a single authoritative contract surface.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` validates only non-empty ID for StarTram entries, with no explicit compatibility/stability metadata to enforce policy stated in README-linked governance docs.

Runtime seams are split into many callback-bag structs and then re-wrapped repeatedly (for example `importerRuntime` -> `initRuntime/workflowRuntime/publishRuntime` plus `resolveImporterRuntimeFor*` scope resolvers in `goseg/importer/importer.go`).

`workflowRuntime` embeds multiple orchestration callback groups and still re-resolves/validates on many call paths (`newWorkflowRuntime` + `resolveWorkflowRuntime`) in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`.

`StartupRuntime` and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` expose large callback surfaces with additional spec indirection (`startBackgroundServiceSpecs`, `startupServiceSpecs`) in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`.

`docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines many narrowly sliced ops structs that are mostly function-field pass-throughs to existing package functions, increasing navigation cost for behavior changes.

Docker SDK types are imported directly across multiple subsystems (`docker/network`, `docker/orchestration/wireguard`, `docker/orchestration/subsystem`, `docker/lifecycle`, `routines/logstream`) instead of being contained behind one adapter boundary.
The codebase mixes a wrapper (`groundseg/dockerclient`) with direct `github.com/docker/docker/*` imports in the same reviewed slice, creating two dependency styles and a larger upgrade blast radius.

Cross-module API signatures expose external Docker SDK types (`container.Config`, `container.HostConfig`, Docker events) deep in internal runtime contracts, coupling orchestration/business modules to vendor package shapes.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows very large flat directories for the reviewed areas: goseg/shipworkflow/ has 45 files, goseg/system/ has 42 files, and goseg/config/ has 41 files (all above the >10 mixed-concern overload threshold in the rubric).

Within goseg/config/, bootstrap.go handles startup/bootstrap flow while config.go defines broad runtime context/state snapshots and multiple settings model types, indicating coarse placement of distinct concerns in one package root.

Within goseg/system/, apt.go is a pure forwarding facade into a deeper package (groundseg/system/maintenance/apt), but remains at system root beside unrelated system concerns, which blurs boundary ownership.

Test-placement drift appears in reviewed files: goseg/system/broadcast_info_test.go and goseg/config/bootstrap_test.go are placeholder-only tests, while separate coverage smoke files (goseg/system/coverage_smoke_test.go, goseg/shipworkflow/coverage_smoke_test.go) add root-level test noise rather than concern-aligned placement.

`goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` has good positive/negative unit coverage for parse/process/connect error paths, but governance compatibility is asserted indirectly through one fixture lookup and not as a broader contract/runtime matrix.
The test suite still contains literal protocol values (for example `payload.Type != "c2c"`) mixed with constant-based checks, which increases drift risk when contracts evolve across packages.

README.md defines backend/frontend quality gates and governance references, but the assigned tests do not verify that contract catalog semantics and runtime action parsing remain aligned across all declared protocol contract entries.

Multiple runtime-oriented modules rely on mutable package globals plus function-pointer seams (e.g., access point defaults and seam functions in goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go; orchestration seam registries in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go; command/runtime globals in goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go), creating hidden process-wide state instead of explicit dependency boundaries.

Transition and action APIs repeat namespace/operation-specific wrappers around common generic behavior (goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go and goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go), indicating duplication at the structural pattern level rather than data-driven dispatch.

Tests repeatedly restore mutable globals to isolate behavior (goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go, goseg/handler/ship/urbit_test.go, goseg/config/config_test.go), which signals production design coupling to global state.

`system.ConfChannel()` returns `chan string`, while `config.ConfChannel()` is a side-effect starter function with no return; same API name represents opposite behaviors across adjacent config/system flow code.

`startuporchestrator` wires config event startup using `config.StartConfEventLoop(ctx, system.ConfChannel())`, while config runtime defaults call `system.ConfChannel()` internally, creating overlapping entrypoints for the same lifecycle.
`broadcast.BroadcastLoop()` and `broadcast.StartBroadcastLoop()` are both public and currently do the same thing, which duplicates boundary API for one lifecycle action.

Error string style is inconsistent across sibling orchestration modules: `subsystem_health.go` uses capitalized messages (e.g., "Failed to ...") while nearby files use lowercase-leading Go-style error messages.
Wireguard artifact errors include capitalized copy prefixes (`"Failed to copy WG config file to volume"`) while other docker/orchestration paths use lowercase sentence style.
Naming style mixes short legacy tokens (`ctype`, `Wg`, `Mc`) with expanded terms (`containerType`, `Wireguard`, `MinIO`) in adjacent runtime/lifecycle APIs.
`protocol/actions` initialization failures panic (`ensureActionContractsInitialized`) even though its public parse/marshal APIs otherwise return typed errors.
Tests for action parsing and C2C flow assert recoverable error behavior, but initialization failure path bypasses those contracts and can terminate process instead of returning domain errors.
Error handling in orchestration uses return-wrapped errors broadly, so panic-as-control-flow in protocol/actions is an outlier relative to surrounding runtime modules.
Acronym vocabulary is inconsistent in core runtime seams: `WiFi` vs `Wifi` (`NewWiFiRuntime`, `NewWifiClient`, `WifiInfoTicker`) in the same subsystem.
Wireguard naming drifts between `WG`, `Wg`, and `Wireguard` across runtime fields and methods (`CreateDefaultWGConfFn`, `GetWgConfFn`, `WireguardRuntime`).
MinIO identifiers split across `Minio*`, `MinIO*`, and `Mc*` (`MinioVersion`, `SetMinIOPasswordFn`, `McConfig`), reducing discoverability and grep-ability.

findings.summary: Contract catalogs rely on mutable package globals, creating cross-module state coupling risk
evidence: `protocolContractSpecs` and `startramContractSpecs` are package-level `var` slices initialized at import time.
Validation/catalog functions consume these shared vars directly, so any in-package mutation changes contract resolution behavior globally.
findings.suggestion: Make catalog specs immutable by construction: replace mutable package vars with function-local literals or expose read-only copies (defensive clone before use). Keep canonical contract descriptors in unmodifiable data structures so runtime and tests cannot mutate boundary-defining metadata.

findings.summary: C2C tests mix raw literals and canonical action contracts, weakening boundary integrity
evidence: Test code checks `payload.Type` against raw string `"c2c"` instead of a canonical constant.
The same test file separately validates action fixtures/contracts via `conformance.ActionContractFixtures()` and `actions.ParseC2CAction`, showing duplicated contract authority paths in one flow.
findings.suggestion: Standardize all protocol identity checks in the test flow on canonical constants/contracts (namespace and action) from the protocol/actions contract boundary. Remove raw protocol literals from assertions/builders to prevent drift when contract tokens evolve.

findings.summary: Runtime callback-bag layering adds high indirection with limited behavioral leverage
evidence: Importer introduces multiple near-identity runtime projections (`initRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, `publishRuntime`) and scope-specific resolvers, then repeats validation by scope.
Ship workflow re-resolves the runtime in many helpers even when already resolved, adding repeated seam plumbing before doing domain work.
Startup orchestrator carries very wide callback structs plus secondary service-spec metadata indirection, making changes require edits in several abstraction layers.
Runtime ops file contains many tiny function-field containers that map almost 1:1 to package functions, increasing call-depth without policy translation.
findings.suggestion: Collapse runtime seams into fewer role-based interfaces (for example `ImportRuntime`, `WorkflowRuntime`, `StartupRuntime`) with explicit method sets, and resolve/validate once at subsystem entrypoints. Remove per-scope projection wrappers when they do not enforce distinct policy.

findings.summary: Process-wide default runtimes create hidden coupling and dual invocation styles
evidence: `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and `defaultBroadcastLoopController` are package globals while the same package also supports explicit runtime injection.
`defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime` and `defaultEventRuntime` are global shared runtime instances; callers can also pass custom runtimes.
This mixed model (global singleton plus injectable runtime) creates implicit shared state paths that are hard to reason about in concurrent/test scenarios.
findings.suggestion: Standardize each subsystem on explicit runtime ownership (construct once in orchestrator and inject) and keep package-level defaults only as thin deprecated compatibility wrappers that delegate to an injected instance.

findings.summary: Docker SDK dependency is diffused across modules, increasing upgrade and coupling risk
evidence: Multiple modules directly import Docker SDK packages (`api/types/container`, `api/types/events`, `client`) and embed those types in internal logic.
Some modules use `groundseg/dockerclient.New` while others still import Docker client/types directly, yielding inconsistent dependency boundaries.
Internal orchestration and lifecycle code now depends on vendor type shapes, so SDK changes propagate broadly.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single internal Docker adapter package that owns SDK imports and translates SDK types into project-local DTOs/events; refactor consumer modules to depend on adapter interfaces and internal models only.

findings.summary: Core directories are over-flat, mixing multiple concerns in single package roots.
evidence: directory_profiles reports goseg/config/ (41 files), goseg/shipworkflow/ (45 files), and goseg/system/ (42 files), which exceeds the >10-file mixed-concern threshold for split consideration.

goseg/config/bootstrap.go contains initialization/bootstrap orchestration while goseg/config/config.go contains runtime-context/state models and global config storage, suggesting distinct change boundaries co-located at root.
goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go is lifecycle-specific but sits in a very broad package root with many workflow domains.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create concern folders `goseg/config/runtime/`, `goseg/config/bootstrap/`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle/`; (2) move lowest-dependency files first (`config.go` runtime/context types, then `bootstrap.go` init flow, then `lifecycle.go`), keeping temporary shims in original packages; (3) update imports via `gofmt -w` on moved files and `goimports -w goseg/config goseg/shipworkflow`; (4) validate with `go test ./goseg/config ./goseg/shipworkflow` and `go test ./...` before removing shims.

findings.summary: APT API lives as a root-level shim instead of with maintenance ownership.
evidence: goseg/system/apt.go only delegates three functions to `groundseg/system/maintenance/apt` and contains no system-root-specific policy.
The existence of a dedicated maintenance apt package plus a root shim indicates incomplete placement migration and unclear ownership boundary at package root.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) establish `goseg/system/maintenance` as the canonical owner and keep `goseg/system/apt.go` as temporary compatibility shim with deprecation comment; (2) migrate direct callers to `groundseg/system/maintenance/apt` (or a `maintenance` facade package) in small batches; (3) run import updates with `goimports -w goseg/system goseg/handler goseg/broadcast`; (4) validate with `go test ./goseg/system ./goseg/system/maintenance/...` and then remove the root shim once callers are migrated.

findings.summary: Placeholder and smoke tests are scattered in package roots, reducing navigability.
evidence: goseg/system/broadcast_info_test.go and goseg/config/bootstrap_test.go are placeholder-only test files and do not validate concrete behavior.
Smoke import tests are duplicated as root-level one-off files in multiple packages, adding file-count noise in already large directories.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create a consistent compile/smoke test location such as `goseg/<pkg>/internaltest/` or one unified `smoke_import_test.go` per package; (2) move/remove placeholder tests first, then consolidate smoke tests into the chosen location and naming convention; (3) update package declarations/imports and run `gofmt -w` plus `goimports -w goseg/system goseg/config goseg/shipworkflow`; (4) validate with targeted runs `go test ./goseg/system ./goseg/config ./goseg/shipworkflow` and full `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Governance policy is documented across modules but not enforced through a shared executable contract schema.
evidence: README references multiple governance policy files for runtime boundaries.

Protocol and StarTram contract specs validate local IDs/prefixes, but do not carry explicit compatibility fields (for example stability level, version epoch, owner module) to make governance checks machine-enforceable.
This leaves architecture contracts split between prose docs and isolated validators.
findings.suggestion: Add shared governance metadata to contract entries (for example `Stability`, `SinceVersion`, `OwnerModule`, `Deprecates`) and extend `validateContractFamilySpecs` to enforce required metadata plus cross-family compatibility rules; then expose one conformance entrypoint consumed by runtime modules and CI so policy and code remain in lockstep.

findings.summary: Protocol action identity is duplicated across `contracts` and `actions` modules, relying on convention-level synchronization.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` defines `ActionC2CConnect` as a local contract verb literal and builds contract IDs from it.
`wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` exercises runtime behavior through `actions.ActionC2CConnect`, not through a single shared contract source type.
Alignment is tested, but compile-time ownership of action identity is still split.
findings.suggestion: Make one module authoritative for action identity (prefer `protocol/actions`), and have contract catalog builders import typed action descriptors from that source instead of redefining literals; keep conformance tests as regression checks, not primary synchronization.

findings.summary: Coverage validates one C2C fixture path but lacks a full contract-to-runtime conformance matrix.
evidence: `TestC2CPayloadAndContractsStayAligned` checks a single C2C connect fixture.
No assigned test enumerates all protocol contract specs and proves every declared action has runtime parse/handling coverage and expected failure semantics.
StarTram contract file defines canonical error contract, but no assigned tests assert runtime emission paths remain bound to that canonical contract.
findings.suggestion: Create a table-driven conformance suite that iterates the contract catalog and asserts parser acceptance/rejection, envelope type behavior, and canonical error mapping for each declared contract; fail CI on any unmapped contract entry.

findings.summary: WiFi C2C tests remain in an overloaded `system` root package despite existing WiFi subpackages and documented subsystem governance.
evidence: `directory_profiles` reports `goseg/system/` with 42 files and mixed responsibilities.
WiFi-specific subfolders (`goseg/system/wifi/service/`, `goseg/system/wifi/transport/`) already exist, indicating intended modularization.
`wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` is still at `goseg/system/` root, reducing ownership clarity and making WiFi governance harder to navigate.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1: create/standardize `goseg/system/wifi/flow/` (or `goseg/system/wifi/c2c/`) as the C2C runtime boundary; Stage 2: move WiFi C2C runtime files and `wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` there before moving generic adapters; Stage 3: update package declarations/imports with `goimports ./...`; Stage 4: validate with `go test ./goseg/system/...` and `go test ./goseg/protocol/...`; Stage 5: run `go test ./...` to confirm no boundary regressions.

findings.summary: Core runtime paths depend on mutable package globals and function seams, weakening explicit ownership boundaries.
evidence: goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go keeps mutable process-level defaults (`wlan`, `inet`, `ip`, `password`, `hostapdConfigPath`) and resolves behavior through runtime function fields set/normalized at call time.
goseg/config/config.go manages process context through global default functions and atomic pointer state (`runtimeContextValue`) with global setters.

goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go exposes mutable package vars for runtime behavior (`urbitTransitionRuntimeFactory`, `runTransitionedOperationFn`, `urbitTransitionRunners`, command map), allowing cross-test and cross-call mutation.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit runtime objects passed through entrypoints (constructor-injected dependencies) and restrict package globals to immutable constants; migrate mutable seams behind internal test-only builders so behavior wiring is explicit per subsystem.

findings.summary: Lifecycle entrypoints duplicate wrapper logic instead of centralizing one coherent entry contract.
evidence: goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go duplicates the same resolved-call path in both `StartWithRuntime` and `startWithRuntime`, and similarly `StopWithRuntime`/`stopWithRuntime`.

goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go exposes paired wrappers (`StartDockerSubsystem` -> `startDockerSubsystemWithRuntime`, `StartDockerHealthLoops` -> `startDockerHealthLoop`) that repeat defaulting/forwarding patterns.
findings.suggestion: Collapse public/private wrappers into a single canonical implementation per lifecycle operation, with one adapter only for default runtime construction; remove duplicate forwarding functions to reduce maintenance drift.

findings.summary: Protocol/transition layers repeat operation-specific wrappers around generic mechanics instead of using a data-driven registration model.
evidence: goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go has many near-identical builders/wrappers (`buildHandleLoomSteps`, `buildHandleSnapTimeSteps`, toggle builders) that differ mainly by small setter/error strings.

goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go repeats namespace-specific wrapper functions (`ParseUploadAction`, `ParseC2CAction`, `SupportedUploadActions`, `SupportedC2CActions`, deprecated/active helpers) over shared generic internals.
findings.suggestion: Define declarative registries for transitions/actions (operation metadata + handlers) and generate or iterate common wrapper behavior from that registry, keeping only domain-specific deltas in per-operation entries.

findings.summary: Test suites rely heavily on resetting production globals, indicating production responsibilities are not cleanly encapsulated.
evidence: goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go repeatedly calls global reset helpers (`resetParameterGlobalsForTest`, `resetRouterGlobalsForTest`) for package-level state.
goseg/handler/ship/urbit_test.go repeatedly snapshots/restores package variables (`urbitCommands`, seam funcs) via cleanup hooks.
goseg/config/config_test.go repeatedly restores mutable globals (`isEMMCMachine`, `mkdirAllFn`, `configMerger`, runtime context setters) in each test.
findings.suggestion: Move mutable runtime/seam state into per-test instances (constructed services) and test through instance APIs; keep package-level mutable seams only as thin compatibility shims during migration.

findings.summary: The same `ConfChannel` name exposes incompatible contracts (channel accessor vs event-loop starter), creating a misleading API boundary.
evidence: `system.ConfChannel(state ...*wifiRuntimeState) chan string` returns a channel and is used as data source.
`config.ConfChannel()` starts an event loop and returns nothing, despite sharing the same noun/verb shape.
Startup wiring uses both patterns in one path (`StartConfEventLoop(ctx, system.ConfChannel())`), making call intent ambiguous.
findings.suggestion: Split the APIs into explicit names by role, e.g. `system.ConfigEventChannel()` and `config.StartConfigEventLoopFromSystemChannel(...)`; deprecate/bridge legacy names with clear comments and one migration path.

findings.summary: Broadcast lifecycle exports duplicate public starters with identical behavior, increasing API surface without semantic distinction.
evidence: `BroadcastLoop()` and `StartBroadcastLoop()` both call `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime(newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithDefault())`.
No behavioral or contract difference is documented between the two exported entrypoints.
findings.suggestion: Keep one canonical public starter and make the other deprecated alias (or remove it), then update tests/callers to the canonical API.

findings.summary: Protocol action initialization panics on contract load errors, violating the package’s otherwise error-returning API behavior.
evidence: `ensureActionContractsInitialized()` panics when `initializeActionContracts()` fails.
Public API methods in the same package (`ParseAction`, `ParseC2CAction`, `MarshalC2CPayload`) return errors and are tested as recoverable failures.
Downstream C2C handling tests assume parse-time errors, but init-time failures can hard-crash instead.
findings.suggestion: Replace panic with explicit initialization error propagation: either add `Init()` returning error and gate APIs on init state, or return deterministic initialization errors from each exported accessor/parse function.

findings.summary: Error message casing/style is inconsistent across orchestration modules, creating a style island that weakens predictable logging and diagnostics.
evidence: `subsystem_health.go` returns capitalized errors like "Failed to get statuses..." and "Failed to retrieve ship information...".
Nearby modules use lowercase-leading wrapped errors (`"resolve container config..."`, `"unable to ..."`).
Wireguard copy-prefix strings are capitalized (`"Failed to copy WG config file to volume"`) while most subsystem errors are lowercase.
findings.suggestion: Adopt one repo-wide error-string convention (lowercase-leading, no trailing punctuation) and normalize all returned/wrapped error prefixes in docker orchestration and lifecycle modules.

findings.summary: Acronym naming drifts (`WiFi/Wifi`, `WG/Wg/Wireguard`, `Minio/MinIO/Mc`) reduce consistency and make APIs harder to navigate.
evidence: Wi-Fi runtime uses both `WiFi` and `Wifi` spellings in the same API surface.
Wireguard runtime mixes `WG`, `Wg`, and full `Wireguard` in adjacent callback names.
MinIO-related models and callbacks alternate among `Minio*`, `MinIO*`, and `Mc*` names for closely related concerns.
findings.suggestion: Define a naming glossary for acronyms and run a staged rename: canonicalize one form per domain (`WiFi`, `WireGuard` or `Wireguard`, `MinIO`) with compatibility aliases only where required for serialization/backward compatibility.
